agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for this loan application audit trail requirement — arguably one of the canonical use cases for the pattern. The regulatory mandate for immutable history, full field-change tracking, and point-in-time reconstruction aligns directly with event sourcing's core properties: append-only event logs as the source of truth, temporal queries via replay, and immutability by design. However, event sourcing introduces significant operational and cognitive complexity. I recommend adopting event sourcing scoped to the Loan Application aggregate specifically, not platform-wide, combined with CQRS to maintain query performance. A simpler CRUD+audit-log alternative was evaluated but rejected as the weaker option because it makes the audit trail a derived side-effect rather than the authoritative source of truth — a distinction regulators will probe.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Loan Application Command Service","responsibility":"Accepts commands (SubmitApplication, UpdateApplicantInfo, ChangeStatus, AddDocument, etc.), validates business rules against current state, and emits domain events. Rejects invalid state transitions.","boundaries":"Owns all write-path logic for loan applications. Does not serve read queries. Communicates only through domain events."},{"name":"Event Store","responsibility":"Append-only, immutable persistence of all domain events for loan application aggregates. Each event is sequenced per aggregate with a global sequence number. Supports optimistic concurrency via expected version.","boundaries":"Write-once storage. No updates, no deletes. Provides stream-read API (by aggregate ID, by event type, by time range). This is the single source of truth."},{"name":"Loan Application Read Model (Projection)","responsibility":"Subscribes to domain events and maintains a denormalized current-state view of each loan application optimized for queries. Replaces the existing loan_applications table semantics.","boundaries":"Eventually consistent with the event store. Disposable and rebuildable from events at any time."},{"name":"Audit Trail Projection","responsibility":"Subscribes to domain events and builds a human-readable audit log with who/what/when for compliance queries. Supports field-level diff rendering and point-in-time state reconstruction.","boundaries":"Read-only projection. Can be rebuilt from events. Optimized for compliance team queries (by date range, by user, by field, by application)."},{"name":"Snapshot Service","responsibility":"Periodically captures materialized aggregate state at a known event version to avoid full replay on every command. Snapshots are an optimization, not the source of truth.","boundaries":"Works alongside the event store. Snapshots can be discarded and regenerated without data loss."},{"name":"Point-in-Time Reconstruction Service","responsibility":"Given an application ID and a timestamp, replays events up to that point to reconstruct exact state. Serves compliance and dispute resolution use cases.","boundaries":"Stateless service. Reads from event store. May leverage snapshots for performance."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Loan Application Command Service","contract":"HTTP POST/PUT commands with command type, payload, and authenticated user context (JWT with user ID, role). Returns command acceptance or rejection.","coupling_type":"loose"},{"from":"Loan Application Command Service","to":"Event Store","contract":"Append(aggregateId, expectedVersion, List<DomainEvent>). Each event includes: eventId (UUID), aggregateId, eventType, payload (JSON), timestamp (UTC), causationId, correlationId, actor (userId + role).","coupling_type":"tight (intentional — command service owns the event store contract)"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Loan Application Read Model (Projection)","contract":"Event subscription via ordered event stream. Projection tracks its checkpoint position. At-least-once delivery with idempotent handlers.","coupling_type":"loose (async, event-driven)"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Audit Trail Projection","contract":"Same event subscription mechanism. Audit projection builds field-level change records with before/after values derived from sequential event application.","coupling_type":"loose (async, event-driven)"},{"from":"Compliance UI","to":"Audit Trail Projection","contract":"Query API: GET /audit/{applicationId}?from=&to=&field=&actor=. Returns ordered list of changes with full context.","coupling_type":"loose"},{"from":"Compliance UI","to":"Point-in-Time Reconstruction Service","contract":"GET /applications/{id}/state-at?timestamp=ISO8601. Returns full application state as it existed at that moment.","coupling_type":"loose"}],"data_flow":"1. User submits a command (e.g., UpdateApplicantIncome) via API. 2. Command Service loads current aggregate state by replaying events from the Event Store (or from latest snapshot + subsequent events). 3. Aggregate validates the command against business rules and the current state. 4. If valid, new domain event(s) are appended to the Event Store with optimistic concurrency check. 5. Event Store publishes events to subscribers. 6. Read Model Projection updates current-state query tables. 7. Audit Trail Projection appends human-readable audit records with field-level diffs. 8. All projections are eventually consistent but the Event Store is immediately consistent and durable.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event Sourcing","rationale":"The core pattern. Stores state as an immutable sequence of domain events rather than mutable current-state rows. Directly satisfies all three compliance requirements: full history (events are the history), point-in-time reconstruction (replay to any event), immutability (append-only store). For financial services audit, making the event log the source of truth rather than a derived artifact is architecturally and legally stronger."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Separates the write model (event-sourced aggregates) from read models (projections). Essential companion to event sourcing — without CQRS, query performance would require full event replay on every read. Multiple projections serve different consumers (application UI vs compliance team) with optimized schemas."},{"pattern":"Domain Events","rationale":"Events like LoanApplicationSubmitted, ApplicantIncomeUpdated, ApplicationStatusChanged, UnderwriterAssigned carry semantic meaning. They are not generic 'field changed' records — they encode business intent, which is far more valuable for audit than raw diffs."},{"pattern":"Aggregate (DDD)","rationale":"The Loan Application is the aggregate root with a clear consistency boundary. All mutations go through the aggregate, which enforces invariants (e.g., cannot approve an application in 'rejected' status, cannot modify a closed application)."},{"pattern":"Optimistic Concurrency","rationale":"Event store uses expected version numbers to prevent conflicting writes to the same aggregate. Critical for financial data integrity."},{"pattern":"Snapshot","rationale":"Performance optimization for aggregates with long event histories. Loan applications may accumulate hundreds of events over their lifecycle. Snapshots prevent replay latency from degrading command processing."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Loan Application Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["LoanApplication — aggregate root. Encapsulates application state, status lifecycle, applicant info, loan terms, documents, and underwriting decisions. Events: LoanApplicationSubmitted, ApplicantInfoUpdated, LoanTermsModified, DocumentAttached, ApplicationStatusChanged, UnderwriterAssigned, ApplicationApproved, ApplicationRejected, ApplicationWithdrawn."]},{"name":"Identity & Access Management","classification":"generic","aggregates":["User — provides actor identity (userId, role, name) that is embedded in every event for audit attribution. Use existing IAM system; do not rebuild."]},{"name":"Compliance & Audit","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["No aggregates of its own — this context consumes events from Loan Application Management and builds read-optimized audit projections. It owns the audit query API and compliance reporting."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Loan Application Management","downstream":"Compliance & Audit","relationship":"Published Language — Loan Application Management publishes a well-defined set of domain events. Compliance & Audit subscribes and interprets them. The event schema is the published language contract."},{"upstream":"Identity & Access Management","downstream":"Loan Application Management","relationship":"Conformist — Loan Application Management accepts the user identity model as-is from IAM (userId, role). No translation needed; the IAM model is simple and stable enough."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Adopt event sourcing instead of CRUD + separate audit log table","gains":"Audit trail is the source of truth, not a derived artifact — eliminates drift risk. Point-in-time reconstruction is a natural capability, not a complex retrofit. Immutability is architectural, not policy-based (no UPDATE/DELETE on event store). Regulators get stronger guarantees. Business intent is captured in event types, not just field diffs.","sacrifices":"Higher implementation complexity. Team must learn event sourcing concepts (aggregates, projections, idempotency). Debugging requires understanding event replay. Existing CRUD codebase must be significantly refactored. Event schema evolution requires careful versioning strategy.","alternatives_considered":["CRUD + immutable audit_log table with triggers or application-level logging. Simpler to implement but audit log is a side-effect that can drift from actual state. Triggers can be bypassed. Application-level logging can be forgotten. Point-in-time reconstruction requires replaying audit log entries, which is essentially event sourcing without the architectural clarity.","Bitemporal tables (e.g., SQL Server temporal tables, PostgreSQL temporal extensions). Database-native versioning gives point-in-time queries cheaply. However, captures data changes, not business intent. 'Income changed from X to Y' is less useful than 'ApplicantIncomeUpdated due to corrected tax return'. Also vendor-locks the audit mechanism to database features.","Change Data Capture (CDC) from database WAL. Captures all changes at the database level. Very low application-level effort. But captures technical changes, not domain events. Schema changes break CDC consumers. No business intent. Useful as a complementary mechanism, not a primary audit strategy."]},{"decision":"Scope event sourcing to Loan Application aggregate only, not platform-wide","gains":"Limits blast radius of complexity. Team can learn ES on one bounded context. Other parts of the platform remain CRUD where appropriate. Faster time to production.","sacrifices":"Two persistence paradigms in one system increases cognitive load. Integration between ES and CRUD contexts requires care. Some developers may push to standardize on one approach prematurely.","alternatives_considered":["Full platform event sourcing — rejected as premature. Not all domains have the same auditability requirements. Over-engineering non-audit-critical areas wastes effort."]},{"decision":"Use domain events with business semantics rather than generic FieldChanged events","gains":"Audit log captures WHY changes happened, not just WHAT changed. ApplicationStatusChanged{from:SUBMITTED,to:UNDER_REVIEW,reason:AUTO_ROUTED} is dramatically more useful for compliance than status='UNDER_REVIEW'. Enables business analytics on event streams.","sacrifices":"More event types to define and maintain. Each new business action requires a new event type. Developers must think carefully about event design.","alternatives_considered":["Generic FieldChanged(entity, field, oldValue, newValue) events — simpler to implement but loses all business context. Compliance team would get a spreadsheet of field diffs with no semantic meaning."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Read model projections","evolution_path":"New projections can be added at any time by replaying the event store. If compliance requirements change (new reports, new query patterns), build a new projection without touching the write path. Old projections can be deprecated independently."},{"area":"Event consumers","evolution_path":"New downstream systems (fraud detection, analytics, ML models) can subscribe to the event stream without modifying the core loan application service. The event store becomes a reusable integration backbone."},{"area":"Event enrichment and transformation","evolution_path":"An Anti-Corruption Layer or event transformation service can be added between the event store and external consumers to translate internal events into published integration events with a stable external schema."},{"area":"Storage technology","evolution_path":"The event store interface can be implemented over different backends: PostgreSQL with an events table initially, migrated to EventStoreDB or Apache Kafka for scale later. The abstraction boundary is the append/read stream interface."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event schema (published events)","reason":"Once events are persisted, their schema is immutable. You cannot change the structure of LoanApplicationSubmitted v1 events that are already stored. Schema evolution must use versioning (upcasting) or new event types. This is intentional rigidity — it protects the audit trail's integrity — but requires disciplined event design upfront."},{"area":"Aggregate consistency boundary","reason":"The LoanApplication aggregate boundary determines what is transactionally consistent. Changing this boundary later (splitting or merging aggregates) requires event migration, which is non-trivial. Get the aggregate boundary right early."},{"area":"Event store immutability","reason":"By design, events cannot be modified or deleted. GDPR right-to-erasure requires a crypto-shredding strategy (encrypt PII with per-entity keys, destroy keys on erasure request) rather than event deletion. This must be designed in from day one, not retrofitted."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Team lacks event sourcing experience, leading to poor event design, projection bugs, or misunderstood consistency model","severity":"high","mitigation":"Invest in training before implementation. Start with a spike/prototype on a non-critical path. Pair with an ES-experienced consultant for the first iteration. Establish event design review as a mandatory process."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution becomes painful as business requirements change","severity":"high","mitigation":"Adopt an upcasting strategy from day one. Version all events (v1, v2). Write upcasters that transform old event versions to current version during replay. Use a schema registry. Enforce backward compatibility rules in CI."},{"risk":"GDPR/data privacy conflict with immutable event store containing PII","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement crypto-shredding: encrypt all PII fields in events with a per-applicant encryption key stored in a separate key vault. On erasure request, destroy the key. Event structure remains intact but PII becomes unrecoverable. Design this into the event serialization layer from the start."},{"risk":"Projection lag causes stale reads, confusing users or causing duplicate commands","severity":"medium","mitigation":"For the application UI, implement read-your-own-writes: after a command is accepted, the response includes the new version number; the UI polls the read model until that version is reflected, or uses the command response optimistically. For compliance queries, eventual consistency is acceptable (seconds of lag)."},{"risk":"Event store grows large over time, degrading replay performance","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Implement snapshots every N events per aggregate (e.g., every 50 events). For projections, track checkpoints so they never replay from zero. Archive old events to cold storage after a retention period if regulations allow (verify retention requirements first)."},{"risk":"Dual persistence paradigms (ES for loans, CRUD elsewhere) increase cognitive overhead","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Clear documentation and architectural decision records. Establish a clear boundary: loan application writes go through the ES path, everything else remains CRUD. No mixing within a single bounded context."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (4-6 weeks): Build the event store on PostgreSQL using an events table (aggregate_id, version, event_type, payload JSONB, timestamp, actor_id, correlation_id). Implement the LoanApplication aggregate with 3-5 core events. Build the current-state projection to replace existing loan_applications table reads. Run in parallel with existing system for validation. Phase 2 (3-4 weeks): Build the audit trail projection and compliance query API. Implement point-in-time reconstruction. Implement crypto-shredding for PII. Phase 3 (2-3 weeks): Add snapshot support. Performance test with realistic event volumes. Cut over from old system. Decommission update-in-place writes.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/loan-application/domain/events/ — one file per event type (LoanApplicationSubmitted.ts, ApplicationStatusChanged.ts, etc.)","src/loan-application/domain/LoanApplicationAggregate.ts — aggregate root with apply() methods for each event","src/loan-application/infrastructure/PostgresEventStore.ts — append/read implementation over PostgreSQL","src/loan-application/infrastructure/event-schema/ — event JSON schemas + upcasters for versioning","src/loan-application/application/commands/ — command handlers (SubmitApplicationHandler.ts, UpdateStatusHandler.ts)","src/loan-application/projections/CurrentStateProjection.ts — replaces loan_applications table reads","src/loan-application/projections/AuditTrailProjection.ts — compliance-optimized audit view","src/loan-application/queries/PointInTimeReconstructionService.ts","src/loan-application/infrastructure/CryptoShredding.ts — PII encryption/key management integration","db/migrations/ — events table, snapshots table, projection tables, audit_trail table"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use a command handler pattern: Command → Validate → Load Aggregate (from events) → Execute business logic → Emit events → Persist events. Projections are idempotent event handlers that maintain their own checkpoint. All events are immutable value objects. Aggregate enforces invariants; projections are eventually consistent read models. Never query the event store directly from the UI — always go through a projection.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in projections — projections are dumb transformers. All rules live in the aggregate.","Do NOT use the event store as a general-purpose message bus — it is for aggregate persistence. Use a separate message broker for cross-context integration if needed.","Do NOT design events as 'entity snapshots' (e.g., ApplicationUpdated with the full entity). Events should capture what changed and why, not the entire state.","Do NOT skip crypto-shredding design — retrofitting PII encryption into an existing event store is extremely painful.","Do NOT allow direct database writes that bypass the aggregate — this breaks the event sourcing invariant and corrupts the audit trail.","Do NOT version events after the fact — version from v1 on day one, even if it feels unnecessary."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Event sourcing is a strong fit for this specific use case — immutable audit trail with point-in-time reconstruction is ES's sweet spot","CQRS is necessary companion pattern for acceptable read performance","Crypto-shredding is the correct approach for GDPR+immutability tension","Scoping ES to the loan application aggregate is the right boundary call","PostgreSQL is sufficient as initial event store — no need for specialized ES database at this scale"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact aggregate boundary without seeing the existing data model — there may be sub-aggregates (e.g., loan documents as a separate aggregate) that would emerge during detailed domain modeling","Specific event granularity — how many distinct event types vs. coarser events depends on actual compliance reporting requirements that would need compliance team input","Retention and archival requirements — regulations may dictate specific retention periods that affect storage strategy"],"assumptions":["The engineering team has or can acquire sufficient ES expertise with reasonable training investment","PostgreSQL is the existing database and can serve as the event store backend","The loan application domain has enough invariants and business rules to justify aggregate modeling (not pure CRUD)","Compliance requirements are stable enough that the event schema will not undergo radical changes frequently","The platform does not already have an event-driven infrastructure (Kafka, etc.) that would change the integration approach"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for this loan application audit trail requirement — arguably one of the canonical use cases for the pattern. The regulatory mandate for immutable history, full field-change tracking, and point-in-time reconstruction aligns directly with event sourcing's core properties: append-only event logs as the source of truth, temporal queries via replay, and immutability by design. However, event sourcing introduces significant operational
